skill.name: when-debugging-ml-training-use-ml-training-debugger
skill.description: Debug machine learning training failures with a structured workflow for diagnosing loss divergence, overfitting, gradient problems, and slow convergence.
skill.summary: Find root causes in ML training runs and apply focused fixes for convergence, stability, and validation performance.
skill.author: DNYoussef

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported many Ruby or shell backtick findings, but review shows these are Markdown code fences around Python and Markdown examples, not dynamic shell execution. The real concerns are a documented npx command for claude-flow@alpha and local file writes for generated ML reports and histories; no malicious intent, data exfiltration, prompt injection, or weak cryptography was found.

medium_findings.title: External Package Execution Guidance
medium_findings.description: README.md documents running npx claude-flow@alpha to execute the skill. This is relevant to the skill, but it runs an external package from the local environment and should be treated as a supply chain and command execution risk.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The command is explicit and user-facing. It appears legitimate for this workflow, but npx execution of an alpha package is an elevated operational risk.

medium_findings.title: Local File Writes During Debugging Workflow
medium_findings.description: The Python examples write diagnostic_report.md, training_history_fixed.json, and training_comparison.png in the working directory. These writes are expected outputs, but users should review paths to avoid overwriting important files.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The file writes are directly present in the examples and have fixed relative output paths. The behavior is useful, but it changes the local filesystem.

low_findings.title: Markdown Code Fences Misclassified as Backtick Execution
low_findings.description: Most static external command alerts point to triple-backtick Markdown fences around Python or Markdown examples. These fences are documentation syntax and are not Ruby or shell backtick execution.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Manual review confirms these locations are fenced documentation blocks. No shell interpolation, eval, subprocess, or Ruby backtick execution is present in those blocks.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography Alert Is Unsupported
low_findings.description: The high static alert at SKILL.md line 4 has no cryptographic code or weak algorithm. The line is a plain description of ML training debugging.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Keyword review found no cryptographic API, algorithm, password handling, or hashing code. The cited line contains only a descriptive sentence.

dangerous_patterns.title: npx Command Invokes External Tooling
dangerous_patterns.description: The quick start uses npx claude-flow@alpha with model and history arguments. Users should verify the package source before execution.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The command is present and executable. It is not malicious by itself, but it delegates execution to an external package.

dangerous_patterns.title: Workflow Writes Generated Artifacts
dangerous_patterns.description: The workflow writes reports, JSON history, and a plot file to relative paths. These are intended outputs, but they modify files in the working directory.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The write calls and savefig call are explicit. They use fixed relative paths, with no evidence of hidden or sensitive path targeting.

content.value_statement: ML training failures can be hard to diagnose when loss curves, gradients, data quality, and architecture interact. This skill gives Claude, Codex, and Claude Code a structured workflow for finding root causes and testing fixes.
actual_capabilities: Analyzes training history for loss divergence, NaN values, overfitting, and slow convergence.
Checks gradient norms for vanishing, exploding, NaN, and infinite values.
Reviews data issues such as class imbalance, outliers, missing normalization, and label noise.
Suggests targeted fixes for learning rate, regularization, gradient clipping, activations, and data scaling.
Compares before and after training metrics and creates a visual training comparison.
Generates diagnostic reports and optimization recommendations for follow-up work.
limitations: Requires existing model objects, training data, and training history to produce useful analysis.
Examples are framework-oriented and may need changes for non TensorFlow or PyTorch projects.
Suggested fixes still require validation on the target dataset and objective metric.
The workflow can retrain models, which may be slow or costly for large workloads.

use_cases.title: Recover a Failed Training Run
use_cases.description: Diagnose NaN loss, diverging loss, or stalled convergence and choose a targeted fix.

use_cases.title: Reduce Overfitting Before Release
use_cases.description: Compare train and validation metrics, identify regularization gaps, and document the chosen correction.

use_cases.title: Audit Model Training Quality
use_cases.description: Review gradients, data quality, architecture complexity, and before or after metrics during model review.

prompt_templates.title: Diagnose Training Curves
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner triage for a single failed run.
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the ML training debugger to inspect my training history. Identify loss divergence, overfitting, slow convergence, NaN values, and likely root causes.

prompt_templates.title: Find Gradient and Data Issues
prompt_templates.scenario: Intermediate investigation after the first diagnosis.
prompt_templates.prompt: Analyze my model gradients and input data using this workflow. Check for exploding gradients, vanishing gradients, class imbalance, outliers, and missing normalization.

prompt_templates.title: Apply Focused Fixes
prompt_templates.scenario: Advanced repair of model or training configuration.
prompt_templates.prompt: Based on the detected root causes, propose concrete changes to learning rate, regularization, gradient clipping, activations, class weights, or normalization. Explain each change.

prompt_templates.title: Validate and Optimize Results
prompt_templates.scenario: Full workflow for comparing repaired training results.
prompt_templates.prompt: Run the complete validation workflow. Compare before and after metrics, confirm whether validation loss improved, and create final optimization recommendations.

output_examples.input: A training history shows rising validation loss while training loss keeps falling.
output_examples.output: The skill reports likely overfitting, recommends dropout or L2 regularization, and asks for a validation retrain to confirm improvement.

output_examples.input: A model begins producing NaN loss after a few epochs.
output_examples.output: The skill checks gradient health, flags numerical instability, and recommends lower learning rate, gradient clipping, and data range checks.

output_examples.input: A fixed run completes after changing normalization and class weights.
output_examples.output: The skill compares validation metrics, summarizes improvement, and lists next optimization steps for the model owner.

best_practices: Keep original training history and configuration available before applying changes.
Validate one major fix at a time so metric changes have a clear cause.
Review generated files and paths before running examples in an important project directory.
anti_patterns: Changing learning rate, architecture, and data preprocessing together without a baseline comparison.
Treating suggested fixes as final without retraining and checking validation metrics.
Running external commands or package tools without verifying the package source and environment.

faq.question: What training problems does this skill target?
faq.answer: It targets NaN loss, loss divergence, overfitting, slow convergence, gradient instability, and poor validation performance.

faq.question: Does it train models automatically?
faq.answer: It provides workflow steps and example code that can retrain a model when the user chooses to run it.

faq.question: What inputs are most useful?
faq.answer: Training history, validation metrics, model architecture, optimizer settings, and representative training and validation data are most useful.

faq.question: Can it work with PyTorch?
faq.answer: The process applies to PyTorch, but many examples use TensorFlow-style code and may need translation.

faq.question: What files can it create?
faq.answer: The examples can create diagnostic reports, fixed training history, and comparison plots in the working directory.

faq.question: Is it safe for production models?
faq.answer: Use it in a controlled branch or experiment workspace, then validate all changes before production deployment.
